content:
# Matthew Chapter 19 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_019_page_001.jpg, MAT_019_page_002.jpg

### Teaching About Divorce

1. When Jesus had finished saying these things, he left Galilee and went into the region of Judea on the other side of the Jordan.

2. Large crowds followed him, and he healed them there.

3. Some Pharisees came to test him. "Is it legal for a man to divorce his wife for any reason?" they asked.

4. "Haven't you read the Scriptures?" Jesus replied. "From the very beginning, the Creator 'made them male and female'

5. and said, 'For this reason a man will leave his father and mother and be united to his wife, and the two will become one flesh.'

6. Since they are no longer two but one, no one should split apart what God has joined together."

7. "Then why did Moses command that a man could give his wife a written notice of divorce and send her away?" they demanded.

8. Jesus replied, "Moses permitted divorce only because of your hard hearts. But it was not what God originally intended.

9. I tell you this: whoever divorces his wife — unless the marriage is unlawful — and marries someone else commits adultery."

10. His disciples said, "If that's how it is between a husband and wife, it's better not to marry!"

11. Jesus replied, "Not everyone can accept this, but only those God has enabled.

12. Some are born unable to marry. Some have been made that way by others. And some choose not to marry for the sake of the kingdom of heaven. Let anyone who can accept this do so."

### Jesus Blesses the Children

13. People brought their children to Jesus so he could lay hands on them and pray for them. But the disciples scolded them for bothering him.

14. Jesus said, "Let the children come to me. Don't stop them! The kingdom of heaven belongs to people like these."

15. He placed his hands on them and blessed them. Then he moved on.

### The Rich Young Man

16. A man came to Jesus and asked, "Teacher, what good thing must I do to get eternal life?"

17. Jesus replied, "Why ask me what is good? Only God is good. But if you want to enter into life, obey the commandments."

18. "Which ones?" the man asked. Jesus said, "Don't murder. Don't commit adultery. Don't steal. Don't lie under oath.

19. Honor your father and mother. And love your neighbor as yourself."

20. "I've kept all of these," the young man said. "What am I still missing?"

21. Jesus told him, "If you want to be complete, go and sell everything you own and give the money to the poor. You will have treasure in heaven. Then come and follow me."

22. When the young man heard this, he walked away sad, because he had a lot of wealth.

23. Then Jesus said to his disciples, "I tell you the truth, it is very hard for a rich person to enter the kingdom of heaven.

24. Let me say it again — it is easier for a camel to squeeze through the eye of a needle than for a rich person to enter the kingdom of God."

25. The disciples were stunned. "Then who in the world can be saved?" they asked.

26. Jesus looked at them and said, "Humanly speaking, it's impossible. But with God, everything is possible."

27. Then Peter spoke up: "We've given up everything to follow you. What will we get?"

28. Jesus replied, "I tell you the truth: when the world is made new and the Son of Man sits on his glorious throne, you who have followed me will also sit on twelve thrones, judging the twelve tribes of Israel.

29. And everyone who has given up houses or brothers or sisters or father or mother or children or property for my sake will receive a hundred times as much and will inherit eternal life.

30. But many who seem to be first will end up last, and many who seem to be last will end up first."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 8 — "hard hearts":** Jesus makes a radical move here. He appeals behind Moses — behind the Torah itself — to God's original design in creation. Moses didn't command divorce; he permitted it as a concession to human brokenness. Jesus restores the original vision: permanent, unbreakable union.

**Verse 12 — Eunuchs for the kingdom:** This strange saying has three categories. Some are born incapable of marriage. Some are made so by others (a reference to literal castration, common in the ancient world). Some voluntarily choose celibacy for the kingdom's sake. Jesus doesn't command celibacy — he acknowledges it as a legitimate calling for those who can receive it.

**Verse 21 — "If you want to be complete":** The word τέλειος ("perfect/complete") doesn't mean moral flawlessness. It means wholeness — being fully what you were created to be. For this particular man, his wealth is the barrier to wholeness. Jesus diagnoses what stands between him and full devotion.

**Verse 24 — The camel and the needle:** Don't soften this. There is no "Eye of the Needle Gate" in Jerusalem (that's a medieval legend). Jesus means exactly what he says: it is humanly impossible for the rich to enter the kingdom. Only God's power (v.26) can accomplish what human effort cannot. Wealth creates a gravitational pull away from dependence on God.

**Verse 28 — παλιγγενεσία:** "When the world is made new" — this extraordinary word appears only here in the Gospels. It envisions not the destruction of creation but its rebirth. The disciples will participate in the governance of this renewed world.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
